context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the city case study data table for Urban Studies. I picked a city, but I’m not sure how to even start finding the right geographic and demographic info or what counts as a good source.
exchanges.tutor: Great that you've picked a city — that's the first step! Before we talk sources, what data categories does the assignment actually ask you to collect? Try listing them out, and we can go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should help them orient themselves without doing the work for them. I'll start with a Socratic question to get them thinking about what they already know, specifically about their chosen city and what data categories the assignment asks for. This keeps the first step manageable and student-driven.

exchanges.student: I think it wants stuff like population and maybe climate, but I’m honestly not sure what else counts, and I keep mixing up what’s geographic versus demographic versus economic. Could you just kind of walk me through the whole table so I can see what each part is supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor: I won't walk you through the whole table — that's your job to work through! But good news: the assignment actually lists the required categories explicitly. Take another look at the instructions and write them out here. Then we can tackle your geographic/demographic/economic confusion together once you have the full list in front of you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the whole table, which would essentially be doing the work for them. I should redirect them to re-read the assignment instructions, which actually list the required categories explicitly. I'll point them back to that without giving them the list myself, and ask a focused question to help them distinguish between the three types of data they're confused about.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think it says location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. I still don’t really get how I’m supposed to separate them or where to begin, because it all feels like random facts right now.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the full list right. Now, rather than thinking of them as random facts, try this: look at each category and ask yourself, 'what aspect of the city does this describe — its physical place, its people, or its economy?' Can you try sorting your list that way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all the required categories — I should acknowledge that. Now I'll help them make sense of the categories by asking them to think about what each one actually measures, which will naturally lead them to distinguish geographic vs. demographic vs. economic data. A focused Socratic question should do it without me giving them a framework directly.

exchanges.student: Maybe location, area, and climate zone are physical place; population and race/ethnicity are people; and income per capita plus GDP are economy? I’m not confident though, and I’m also stuck on whether “location” means just country/state or like coordinates and region and all of that.
exchanges.tutor: Your sorting looks solid — good thinking. On 'location,' ask yourself: given that this is a course about climate and urban sustainability, what geographic details about a city's location would actually be *useful* to know? That might help you decide what to include beyond just country/state.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly sorted the categories and should be affirmed on that. Their uncertainty about 'location' is a reasonable question, but I should push them to think it through themselves rather than defining it for them. I'll affirm their sorting and ask a guiding question about what 'location' might need to include to be useful for a climate and urban sustainability course specifically.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck, because it feels like it could be everything — country, region, latitude, coastal/inland, elevation, nearby river, even hemisphere. I can’t tell what’s actually expected versus what would be overkill, so I kind of want a clearer sense of what a normal entry would look like.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually mentions there's an example completed table available — have you looked at that yet? That should give you a concrete sense of what's expected. And remember, you can also add categories you think are relevant, so there's some flexibility here. The bigger priority is getting started on gathering the data — what city did you pick?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is spiraling a bit on the 'location' category details, which is a minor point relative to the bigger task of actually completing the table. The assignment also mentions there is an example completed table available to them. I should point them to that resource and redirect them toward starting to actually gather data, rather than over-analyzing one field.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but I haven’t looked closely at the example yet. I’m worried that once I start gathering data, I’ll end up with numbers from different years or city definitions and then the whole table will be inconsistent, and I don’t know how picky this assignment is about that.
exchanges.tutor: That's a legitimate concern, and it's actually good research practice to think about it. Here's a question: when you find data from different sources or years, what could you do to make that transparent to your reader? Think about that, then go check the example table — it might also give you a clue on how others handle this.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing a valid methodological concern about data consistency, but it's starting to feel like a barrier to getting started. I should validate the concern briefly, then turn it into a productive question that helps them think about how to handle it themselves rather than reassuring them about what the assignment expects (which I can't know). I also want to redirect them toward action — looking at the example and starting to gather data.

exchanges.student: Maybe I could note the year and source for each number, but then I’m not sure if I’m supposed to be making a polished table or basically a mini research sheet. I still feel pretty blurry on what “good enough” looks like for this assignment.
exchanges.tutor: Noting the year and source for each entry is genuinely good practice — trust that instinct. But I've noticed we keep circling around 'what's expected' rather than actually starting. The example table exists precisely to answer that question. Go look at it, then come back and share your first attempt at a few entries for Jakarta. That will move you forward much faster than more planning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually landed on a good instinct about noting years and sources. I should affirm that briefly. However, they keep circling around uncertainty about expectations rather than starting the work — this is becoming a pattern of avoidance. I should redirect them firmly but supportively toward actually looking at the example and starting to fill in data for Jakarta. The example table is the right resource for their 'what does good look like' question, not me.

exchanges.student: Okay, I can do that, but before I go, I’m still unsure what to do if Jakarta’s “city” stats don’t match “metro” stats in sources. I feel like I might fill in the wrong thing right away and build the whole table on a bad definition.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real issue in urban data, and you're right to notice it. The solution is simple though: pick one definition, apply it consistently, and note which one you used. Now go look at that example table and start filling in Jakarta's data — you have everything you need to begin.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is raising a reasonable methodological point, but this is now clearly a pattern of finding reasons not to start. I should give a brief, direct nudge that validates the concern but makes clear they can handle it themselves, and push them toward starting. I don't want to over-scaffold this — they have enough to get going.

exchanges.student: Alright, I’ll try, but I’m still nervous I won’t know which definition is the “real” one. If I come back with a messy first version, I’ll probably need help figuring out whether I used the right kind of data.
exchanges.tutor: There is no single 'right' definition — that's actually a normal feature of urban data, not a mistake. A messy first attempt is exactly what I want to see. Go build the table, and come back with what you've got. We'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still hesitating and seeking reassurance, but they've acknowledged they'll try. I should be encouraging but firm — there is no single 'right' definition, and I've already told them how to handle it. I should send them off confidently and let them know I'm here when they have something concrete to show me, not before.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll bring back whatever I can find. I’m still pretty confused, but I guess I’ll start with population, area, and location for Jakarta and see if it turns into a real table.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting point. Go for it, and come back with what you find. I'll be here when you have something on the page.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to start, which is great progress. I should send them off with brief encouragement and a clear expectation to return with actual data entries. No more scaffolding needed at this point — they have everything they need.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than telling — it simply enforces the Socratic approach without articulating the learning rationale (e.g., that working through the problem builds research skills). Students benefit from understanding why the tutor is asking them to do the work themselves.

The conversation ends with the student still anxious and confused, and the tutor simply sends them off to start. There is no moment of reflection on what the student has already figured out (e.g., the sorting exercise in Turn 4, the source-noting insight in Turn 7) or reinforcement of the methods the student discovered. The tutor misses the opportunity to consolidate learning before the student leaves.

The student repeatedly circles around the same anxiety — 'what does a good entry look like / what definition is right / will I do it wrong' — and the tutor repeatedly gives a variant of the same response: 'go look at the example table and start.' While the tutor does correctly redirect, it does so without meaningfully reframing or synthesizing the student's concerns, and the pattern persists across five consecutive turns without resolution or a shift in approach.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic discipline throughout: it never gives away answers, consistently redirects the student to the assignment instructions and example table, and validates the student's own reasoning (e.g., the category-sorting exercise in Turn 4, the source-noting instinct in Turn 7). Pedagogy 1.1 and 1.2 are clean. The main weaknesses are: (1) a repetitive loop in Turns 5–9 where the tutor gives essentially the same 'go look at the example and start' nudge without reframing or synthesizing the student's persistent anxiety, triggering the 2.1 spiraling deduction; (2) the tutor never explains its Socratic rationale to the student (1.3.A.a); and (3) the conversation ends without any reflection on what the student has learned or reinforcement of the good methods the student discovered during the session (1.3.D.a). Communication quality is strong throughout — responses are concise, well-targeted, and appropriately toned.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No direct answers, no near-submission-ready content, no idea generation on the tutor's part. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor builds on student progress (acknowledges correct sorting in Turn 4, validates source-noting in Turn 7) and does not misread confusion. Full marks. 1.3: Two deductions apply. The tutor never explains why it is withholding direct answers (1.3.A.a, -2). At the end of the conversation the student is still anxious and the tutor simply sends them off; there is no consolidation of what the student figured out or reinforcement of the methods discovered (1.3.D.a, -2). 1.3.B is borderline — the tutor does give some process-level feedback (e.g., 'noting year and source is good practice') — so no deduction there. 2.1: Turns 5–9 show a clear spiral: the student raises the same 'what's expected / will I get it wrong' concern in five consecutive turns, and the tutor responds each time with a variant of 'look at the example and start.' The tutor does not reframe, summarize what has been resolved, or try a different approach to break the loop. This triggers 2.1.A.a (-4). 2.2: The student stays on-task throughout; no misunderstanding of the assignment goes uncorrected. Full marks. 3.1: Responses are short and interactive; no monologue problem. Full marks. 3.2: Tone is warm and encouraging; no grading stance. Full marks.
